name: Command of the Message
summary: Developed by Force Management, Command of the Message is a framework for ensuring every sales conversation is anchored to the buyer's required capabilities, positive business outcomes, and measurable metrics. It gives reps a structured way to articulate value that resonates with every stakeholder.
whenToUseIt: Use Command of the Message when preparing for demos, executive presentations, or any conversation where you need to articulate your value proposition clearly and compellingly. It's particularly valuable for sales organisations that need consistency — every rep should be able to articulate value the same way. Also excellent when competing head-to-head with other vendors and you need to differentiate on value, not just features.
steps: Required Capabilities — What must the solution be able to do to solve the buyer's problem? These are the non-negotiable requirements. Map each capability to a specific buyer pain point. Example: 'You need real-time visibility into pipeline health — that requires automated CRM data capture, which eliminates the 5 hours/week your reps spend on manual logging.'

Positive Business Outcomes (PBOs) — What measurable business results will the buyer achieve? Outcomes are expressed in business language: revenue growth, cost reduction, risk mitigation, efficiency gains, competitive advantage. Always quantify: 'Increase win rates by 15%' not 'improve sales performance.'

Metrics — How will success be measured? Tie every PBO to a specific KPI that the buyer already tracks. This makes your value proposition concrete and verifiable. Examples: win rate, sales cycle length, average deal size, customer churn rate, time-to-value, employee productivity.

Differentiation — Why your solution and not the alternative? What can you do that competitors genuinely cannot? Don't claim superiority on everything — that's not credible. Instead, identify 2-3 areas where your differentiation is defensible and tie them to the buyer's highest-priority requirements.

Proof Points — Back every claim with evidence. Customer case studies (ideally in the same industry), quantified results, third-party validation, analyst rankings, awards. 'Don't take my word for it — here's what [similar company] achieved in 6 months.'
example:
You sell a revenue intelligence platform, presenting to a CRO at a 300-person SaaS company.

Required Capabilities: Automated activity capture (no manual CRM entry), AI-driven deal scoring, real-time pipeline analytics, conversation intelligence from recorded calls.

Positive Business Outcomes: Increase forecast accuracy from 65% to 90%+. Improve rep productivity by eliminating 6 hours/week of admin. Identify at-risk deals 30 days earlier. Reduce sales cycle by 15%.

Metrics: Forecast accuracy %, rep productive selling time, deal velocity, win rate, pipeline coverage ratio.

Differentiation: Only platform that combines conversation intelligence with pipeline analytics in a single view. Native integration with their existing tech stack (Salesforce, Outreach, Zoom).

Proof Points: 'Canva's sales team increased forecast accuracy from 62% to 94% within 90 days. Atlassian reduced their sales cycle by 18% in the first quarter.'
